worth_it_explanation: Creative blocking sets which advertisers, categories, and domains a publisher will not accept on its inventory, so it controls the line between protecting the brand and keeping demand flowing. A block list that is stale lets unwanted creative slip through, and a block list that is too broad turns away demand the publisher would have been glad to take. The check is a review of block-list settings the publisher already has, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete blocking decision the publisher can adjust.
intro: Creative blocking on TripleLift decides which advertisers, advertiser categories, and domains a publisher will not allow on its inventory. TripleLift is a sell-side platform (SSP) known for native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats, where the ad sits closer to the publisher's own content than a standard banner does, so the brand-safety stakes of who appears are higher. This subcategory covers the block lists themselves, what they exclude and how broadly, rather than the contextual content categories TripleLift assigns to inventory. These checks confirm that the block lists match the publisher's own standards and that they are not drawn so wide that they suppress revenue the publisher meant to keep.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser, category, and domain block lists do not match the publisher's standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Block lists encode the publisher's brand-safety standards, so when they are stale or never set, creative the publisher would reject can clear on its inventory. On native and branded-content placements, where the ad sits next to the publisher's own content, an unwanted advertiser or category reads as an endorsement, which carries reputational cost beyond the single impression.

educate.base:
A block list is the publisher's stated answer to a simple question. Which advertisers, advertiser categories, and domains will this site refuse to show, regardless of what they bid? On an exchange such as TripleLift, demand arrives through real-time bidding rather than a direct relationship, so the publisher rarely knows in advance which advertiser will win an impression. The block list is the control that holds the publisher's standards in place when the buyer is decided by an auction the publisher does not see.

The OpenRTB protocol that carries these bids defines the blocking attributes a publisher can set. Blocked advertiser domains (badv) name specific advertisers by their domain. Blocked advertiser categories (bcat) name whole categories using the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy, so a publisher can exclude, for example, gambling or alcohol creative in one rule rather than chasing individual advertisers. TripleLift exposes these controls in its platform, and through the TripleLift Prebid adapter a publisher running header bidding can also carry badv and bcat values in the bid request.

The risk this check looks at is drift. A block list reflects the publisher's standards at the moment it was written. Categories change names, new advertiser domains appear, a sponsorship deal makes a previously acceptable category off-limits near certain content, and the list is rarely revisited to match. A list that no longer reflects the publisher's standards lets creative through that the publisher would reject if asked directly, and on a native or branded-content unit that creative carries the visual weight of the publisher's own design.

A few signs point to block lists that do not match the publisher's standards:
  • The block lists have not been reviewed against the publisher's current brand-safety policy within a defined period
  • Category blocks reference an outdated taxonomy version or leave out categories the publisher's policy names as off-limits
  • Known unwanted advertisers appear in delivery reporting despite a domain block that should have caught them
  • No one on the publisher's side can state what the block lists currently exclude or why
You may want to confirm that the advertiser, category, and domain block lists match the publisher's written brand-safety standards. One approach is to read the publisher's policy alongside the active block lists and reconcile the two, then set a review cadence so the lists stay current.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, editorial credibility depends on which advertisers appear next to the reporting, and native units make that adjacency visual rather than incidental. You may want to align the category and advertiser blocks with the newsroom's own standards, for example excluding categories the publication has committed not to run near sensitive coverage, so the block list enforces the editorial policy rather than sitting separate from it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, competitor advertisers and categories that undercut the site's own products are the usual blocking concern because a rival's native ad next to a buying guide works against the page. You may want to confirm the advertiser-domain blocks cover the direct competitors the merchandising team would name, and that the list keeps pace as new competitors enter the category.

investigate.intro: Review the active advertiser, category, and domain block lists against the publisher's written brand-safety standards, and look for any unwanted advertiser slipping through in delivery reporting.

publisher_policy.label: Publisher brand-safety policy
steps: Ask the publisher for its written brand-safety or advertiser-acceptance policy, including any categories or advertisers it has committed not to run.
List the advertiser domains, categories, and domains the policy says should be blocked.
Record when the policy was last updated and who owns it, so the block lists can be reconciled against a current standard.

delivery_reporting.label: Delivery reporting
steps: Pull a recent delivery report that shows which advertisers and categories actually served on the publisher's TripleLift inventory.
Compare the advertisers and categories that served against the publisher's policy list to find any that should have been blocked.
Note any unwanted advertiser or category that cleared despite an intended block, which points to a block list that is missing, stale, or misconfigured.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the publisher's written brand-safety standards alongside the active advertiser, category, and domain block lists, and checks delivery reporting for advertisers or categories that served against policy. A setup where the block lists reconcile with a current policy and reporting shows no unwanted advertisers clearing passes. A setup where the lists are stale, miss categories the policy names, or let blocked advertisers through is a fail recorded with the gaps between policy and block list.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the publisher's brand-safety standards and the active block lists above, then note any unwanted advertiser found in delivery reporting, to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser, category, and domain block lists on {{site_name}}'s TripleLift inventory do not match its written brand-safety standards. Block lists encode the publisher's standards for who may appear, and when they drift, creative the publisher would reject clears on inventory, which carries more weight on native and branded-content units where the ad sits next to the publisher's own content. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling the active advertiser-domain, category, and domain blocks against the current brand-safety policy, confirming the category blocks reference the Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy the policy intends, and setting a review cadence so the lists stay current. After the reconciliation, recheck delivery reporting to confirm no blocked advertiser or category is still clearing.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are drawn broadly enough to suppress revenue
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list that is wider than the publisher's brand-safety standards require turns away demand the publisher would have accepted, so fill rate and revenue fall without protecting anything the publisher cared about. The cost hides because a blocked bid leaves no trace in delivery reporting, which shows what served rather than what was refused.

educate.base:
Blocking has a price. Every advertiser, category, or domain a publisher refuses is demand removed from the auction, and an auction with fewer bidders clears at a lower price or does not clear at all. A block list set to the publisher's actual standards is worth that price because the impressions it turns away were ones the publisher did not want. A block list drawn wider than those standards pays the same price for no protection, and the demand it refuses was demand the publisher would have been glad to take.

Over-broad blocking takes a few shapes. A whole Interactive Advertising Bureau (IAB) Content Taxonomy category gets blocked to keep out one objectionable advertiser within it, removing every other advertiser in the category along with the target. A block list inherited from another property or copied from a template carries exclusions that never applied to this publisher. A one-time response to a single complaint hardens into a permanent block that outlives the reason for it. In each case the list keeps growing and is rarely trimmed. Adding a block feels safe and removing one feels risky.

The reason this is easy to miss is that a blocked bid is invisible. Delivery reporting shows the impressions that served, not the bids that were refused before the auction, so a publisher reading its reports sees no sign of the revenue a wide block list cost. The only way to see the trade is to look at the block list itself and ask, for each entry, whether the publisher's standards actually require it.

A few signs point to blocking that is broader than the standards require:
  • Entire categories are blocked when the concern was a specific advertiser or a narrower subcategory within them
  • The block list was inherited or copied rather than built from this publisher's own policy
  • Entries exist that no one can tie back to a current brand-safety reason
  • Fill rate or bid density on TripleLift inventory is lower than comparable inventory without a clear cause
You may want to review each block-list entry against the publisher's brand-safety standards and remove the ones those standards do not require. One approach is to narrow category-wide blocks to the specific advertisers or subcategories that prompted them, then watch fill rate and revenue to confirm the inventory recovers the demand the wider block had been turning away.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a category block added in response to a single reader complaint can stay in place long after the concern passed, removing a category of demand the newsroom has no standing objection to. You may want to date each category block and revisit the ones tied to one-off events, narrowing them to the specific advertiser where the standard allows the rest of the category back.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, blocking a broad category to keep out a single competitor can also exclude complementary advertisers whose products pair with the site's own. You may want to replace category-wide competitor blocks with advertiser-domain blocks aimed at the named rivals, so the complementary demand in that category stays available.

investigate.intro: Review each block-list entry against the publisher's brand-safety standards to find exclusions broader than those standards require, then check fill rate for a sign the blocking is costing demand.

block_list_review.label: Block-list review
steps: List every advertiser-domain, category, and domain block currently active on the publisher's TripleLift inventory.
For each entry, record the brand-safety reason the publisher can give for it, and flag any entry with no current reason.
Flag every category-wide block whose original concern was a single advertiser or a narrower subcategory. Those are the candidates to narrow.

fill_reporting.label: Fill and bid-density reporting
steps: Build a report showing fill rate and the number of bids per impression on TripleLift inventory.
Compare that inventory against comparable inventory that carries a narrower block list, if one exists, to gauge how much demand the broader blocking removes.
Note any inventory where fill rate or bid density is low without a clear cause, which points to blocking that may be wider than the standards require.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block-list entry with the brand-safety reason behind it and reads fill rate and bid density against comparable inventory. A block list where every entry maps to a current standard, and category-wide blocks exist only where the whole category is genuinely off-limits, passes. A block list carrying entries with no current reason, or category-wide blocks that a narrower advertiser or subcategory block would cover, is a fail recorded with the entries that suppress demand without protecting the publisher.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block-list entry with its brand-safety reason and the fill rate on the affected inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists on {{site_name}}'s TripleLift inventory are drawn broadly enough to turn away demand the publisher's standards do not require it to refuse. Every blocked advertiser, category, or domain removes a bidder from the auction, so a block list wider than the brand-safety policy lowers fill rate and revenue without protecting anything, and the cost stays hidden because a refused bid leaves no trace in delivery reporting. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing each entry against the current standards, narrowing category-wide blocks to the specific advertisers or subcategories that prompted them, and removing entries no current reason supports. After narrowing the lists, {{voice_recommend}} watching fill rate and bid density to confirm the inventory recovers the demand the wider blocks had been refusing.
